name: Suite Utilization
consequences: Immediate: Increase nightly occupancy of suites → Systemic: 20% higher data throughput measured by nights per participant → Strategic: Balances recruitment speed against staffing cost, trading faster enrollment for higher staffing strain.
options: Maintain current 8‑suite occupancy with staggered admissions to keep staffing ratios stable.
Add a 9th suite and implement a rotating shift schedule to absorb higher occupancy.
Deploy a modular, mobile suite that can be rapidly installed or removed to match demand, allowing dynamic scaling without permanent expansion.
review: Controls recruitment speed vs staffing cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider long‑term staff burnout.

name: Event Capture Cadence
consequences: Immediate: Increase frequency of enhanced PSG nights → Systemic: 15% rise in captured parasomnia events per participant, measured by adjudicated event count → Strategic: Improves phenotyping depth but escalates equipment wear and technician workload, trading data richness for operational cost.
options: Keep current schedule of three enhanced nights per stay.
Add a fourth enhanced night on week 3 for participants with low event yield.
Introduce a continuous, low‑burden PSG overlay using wearable EEG during all nights, eliminating discrete enhanced nights.
review: Controls data depth vs operational cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant sleep disruption.

name: Data Annotation Workflow
consequences: Immediate: Implement algorithmic pre‑scoring of events → Systemic: 30% reduction in manual annotation hours, measured by hours per event → Strategic: Lowers labor cost but may introduce bias if algorithm misclassifies, balancing cost savings against data quality.
options: Continue manual annotation by two independent raters with periodic consensus meetings.
Add a single algorithmic pre‑scoring step to flag likely events before human review.
Deploy a fully automated, algorithmic triage that assigns confidence scores and requires human review only for low‑confidence cases.
review: Controls labor cost vs data accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider inter‑rater variability.

name: Participant Recruitment Funnel
consequences: Immediate: Expand recruitment to regional sleep clinics → Systemic: 25% increase in enrollment rate, measured by participants per month → Strategic: Accelerates study power but increases logistical complexity and consent burden, balancing speed against operational overhead.
options: Recruit exclusively through the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic.
Add regional neurologist referrals and a dedicated outreach coordinator.
Launch a multi‑center, cross‑border recruitment network with standardized protocols and multilingual consent materials.
review: Controls enrollment speed vs operational overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural and language barriers.

name: Privacy‑Aware Video Policy
consequences: Immediate: Restrict video capture to non‑bedroom areas → Systemic: 10% reduction in privacy concerns, measured by participant withdrawal rate → Strategic: Enhances participant comfort but limits high‑resolution event capture, balancing ethical compliance against data granularity.
options: Keep current policy of no bedroom video, using only infrared cameras in common areas.
Introduce low‑resolution, privacy‑preserving cameras in bedrooms with blurred faces.
Employ motion‑sensing, depth‑camera technology that captures movement without identifiable visual data.
review: Controls privacy vs data granularity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on event detection sensitivity.

name: Technology‑Tiered Monitoring
consequences: Immediate: Upgrade dry‑electrode headbands to high‑density wireless arrays → Systemic: 20% improvement in signal quality, measured by signal‑to‑noise ratio → Strategic: Enhances event detection accuracy but raises equipment cost and participant comfort, balancing quality against cost.
options: Maintain current dry‑electrode headbands.
Replace with a hybrid wired‑wireless system that offers better fidelity.
Deploy a next‑generation, fully wireless, high‑density EEG system with automatic calibration routines.
review: Controls signal quality vs cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider long‑term maintenance and calibration.

name: Sleep Suite Allocation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Increase suite occupancy to 8 active suites while implementing staggered admissions to preserve safety protocols → Systemic: Participant throughput rises by 25% but safety incident rate increases by 10% due to higher density → Strategic: Balancing higher data yield with elevated safety risk may constrain long‑term scalability, limit regulatory approval, and necessitate additional safety investments.
options: Maintain current 8‑suites occupancy with staggered admissions to preserve safety.
Expand to 10 suites with enhanced safety monitoring systems.
Deploy modular, mobile sleep pods that can be rapidly reconfigured, leveraging autonomous safety sensors.
review: Controls throughput vs. safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider long‑term staff fatigue.

name: Event Detection Sensitivity Calibration
consequences: Immediate: Adjust detection thresholds to a more permissive setting to capture subtle events → Systemic: Sensitivity increases by 15% but false positive rate rises by 20%, leading to a 30% increase in annotation hours → Strategic: Higher event capture enhances phenotyping depth but escalates annotation workload, potentially delaying publication timelines and straining staffing resources.
options: Use conservative threshold settings to limit false positives.
Implement adaptive thresholding based on nightly baseline metrics.
Integrate machine‑learning anomaly detection that continuously refines sensitivity thresholds in real time.
review: Controls sensitivity vs. false positives. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on technician fatigue.

name: Annotation Accuracy Enhancement
consequences: Immediate: Shift to dual‑annotator workflow with adjudication for ambiguous cases → Systemic: Annotation time per night increases by 30% while inter‑rater reliability improves from 0.75 to 0.90 → Strategic: Higher reliability supports robust analyses but raises operational costs, potentially limiting participant enrollment and extending project timelines.
options: Rely on current single‑annotator workflow with periodic quality checks.
Introduce dual‑annotator consensus with adjudication for ambiguous cases.
Adopt a semi‑automated annotation platform that flags high‑confidence segments for rapid review.
review: Controls speed vs. accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider the learning curve for new annotators.

name: Participant Engagement Retention
consequences: Immediate: Increase participant incentives to include travel vouchers and personalized sleep reports → Systemic: Retention rate improves by 20% while cost per participant rises by 15% → Strategic: Higher retention enhances data richness but may strain budget and alter participant demographics, impacting generalizability.
options: Offer standard compensation and flexible scheduling.
Provide additional incentives such as travel vouchers and personalized sleep reports.
Implement a subscription‑based engagement model where participants receive ongoing sleep coaching and data insights.
review: Controls retention vs. cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural differences in incentive perception.

name: Data Sharing Governance Model
consequences: Immediate: Expand data sharing scope to a tiered access platform → Systemic: Data usage increases by 40% while privacy breach risk rises by 5% → Strategic: Broader access accelerates scientific discovery but necessitates robust governance to maintain participant trust and comply with regulations.
options: Share only de‑identified raw sensor data under strict data use agreements.
Release processed datasets with aggregated metrics while restricting raw video.
Offer a tiered data access platform where researchers can request deeper data layers under vetted agreements.
review: Controls openness vs. privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider the administrative burden of managing access requests.

name: Staffing Flexibility Protocol
consequences: Immediate: Shift to rotating, cross‑trained staffing model with remote monitoring support → Systemic: Staffing cost decreases by 12% while coverage reliability improves by 8% → Strategic: Cost savings support expansion but may increase staff turnover risk and affect morale, potentially impacting data quality.
options: Maintain fixed night shift staffing with full coverage.
Introduce part‑time staffing to cover peak periods.
Deploy a rotating, cross‑trained staffing model that leverages remote monitoring support.
review: Controls cost vs. coverage reliability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on staff morale.

name: Sleep Suite Occupancy Optimization
consequences: Immediate: Increase nightly occupancy rate → Systemic: 15% higher data yield per suite, reducing per‑event cost by €200 → Strategic: Balances throughput against risk of over‑crowding, potentially compromising participant comfort and safety.
options: Maintain current 8‑suite capacity with staggered admissions to preserve quiet environment.
Expand to 10 suites by adding modular units, allowing simultaneous admissions while monitoring noise levels.
Deploy mobile sleep pods that can be relocated between rooms, enabling flexible occupancy and rapid scaling.
review: Controls Cost vs. Participant Comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider long‑term maintenance costs of modular units.

name: Event Annotation Turnaround
consequences: Immediate: Reduce annotation time per night by 30% → Systemic: 20% faster data availability, accelerating analysis cycles and reducing overtime costs by €50K annually → Strategic: Enhances research agility but may increase error rates if shortcuts are taken.
options: Continue manual annotation by technicians with periodic quality checks.
Introduce semi‑automated waveform highlighting to guide technicians, reducing review time.
Implement a cloud‑based collaborative annotation platform with real‑time consensus scoring, enabling rapid turnaround and distributed expertise.
review: Controls Quality vs. Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of network latency on real‑time scoring.

name: Participant Safety Protocol
consequences: Immediate: Implement continuous safety monitoring → Systemic: 10% reduction in incident reports, improving regulatory compliance and participant trust → Strategic: Strengthens ethical standing but increases operational complexity and staffing needs.
options: Rely on existing night‑shift technicians to perform routine safety checks.
Add a dedicated safety officer to conduct daily rounds and emergency drills.
Deploy wearable biometric sensors that alert staff to physiological distress in real time, integrating with an automated incident response system.
review: Controls Staffing vs. Safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider the privacy implications of continuous biometric monitoring.

name: Data Integrity Assurance
consequences: Immediate: Implement double‑write redundancy for all sensor streams → Systemic: 99.9% data loss prevention, reducing data recovery costs by €30K per year → Strategic: Guarantees dataset reliability but adds storage overhead and complexity.
options: Store data locally on NAS with nightly encrypted backups to university servers.
Mirror data to a secondary on‑premises server with automated checksum verification.
Utilize a hybrid cloud storage solution with immutable object storage and automated versioning, ensuring tamper‑evidence and auditability.
review: Controls Cost vs. Reliability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory requirements for data residency in Germany.

name: Funding Sustainability Pathway
consequences: Immediate: Secure a multi‑year core grant → Systemic: 25% increase in budget stability, allowing phased expansion of participant cohort → Strategic: Provides financial resilience but may lock the project into specific research priorities.
options: Apply for a single large DFG grant covering all three years.
Pursue a staggered funding strategy with annual DFG renewals and supplemental institutional support.
Develop a consortium model with industry partners and academic institutions, sharing costs and co‑authoring publications.
review: Controls Innovation vs. Funding Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of industry partnership on publication independence.
